metadata.title: The Ten Kinds of Play: Why Genre Is the Wrong Map
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: The Ten Kinds of Play: Why Genre Is the
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Genre labels sort games by surface features, which is the wrong map for understanding play. A better approach names each kind of play by what it actually feels like to the player. Simulation play is about managing a system, not the buildings or objects you see. Chance interrupts these felt experiences, cutting across the categories rather than sitting neatly inside them.

artifactLines: Genre labels sort games by surface features, which is the wrong map for understanding play.
A better approach names each kind of play by what it actually feels like to the player.
Simulation play is about managing a system, not the buildings or objects you see.
Chance interrupts these felt experiences, cutting across the categories rather than sitting neatly inside them.

beats.narration_text: The idea that genre is the wrong map and that I should name games by what they feel like really reframes how I sort my library. I'm stuck on the simulation category, since Cities: Skylines apparently isn't about buildings and the fog isn't physical. Can you (1) tell me what the felt experience of a simulation game actually is if not the objects on screen; (2) give me two other games that feel like Cities: Skylines despite looking nothing alike; (3) show how chance interrupts that feeling?

output: You’re holding a controller, staring at a screen
Game designers Colleen Macklin and John Sharp noticed this fog
The result is a taxonomy of ten kinds of play

beats.narration_text: This is Chapter 3 of their framework. Start naming them by what they feel like.
